text: The video visually highlights the anatomical attachments of the colon, including the splenocolic ligament connecting the colon to the spleen, the phrenocolic ligament, and the gastrocolic ligament, demonstrating their positions and relationships within the surgical field.

The robotic third arm lifts the divided inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) pedicle upward in a twelve o'clock position, separating the thin mesentery from the retroperitoneum. The pancreas is visible, and the splenic vein is exposed, highlighting the anatomical structures in a slender patient.

The robotic third arm lifts the divided inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) pedicle in a twelve o'clock position. The surgeon pushes the pancreas down, maintaining a natural plane to avoid a distal pancreatectomy. The pancreas is pushed down towards the retroperitoneum, while the descending mesocolon is elevated anteriorly. The surgeon divides the ligament of Treitz and pushes down the retroperitoneal attachments and the duodenum, revealing the thin mesocolon. The procedure continues with the division of the lateral attachments of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and proceeds laterally, with assistance pulling the splenic flexure down. The areolar tissue plane is exposed, revealing freed-up retroperitoneum. The frontal colic ligament and lateral attachments of the colon are opened up, showing an avascular retroperitoneum. The omental colic attachment is peeled up, mobilizing the transverse colon up to its mid portion. The medial mesentery is dissected to the border of the middle colic artery, ensuring no mesentery remains to the left or distal to the middle colic artery. The splenic flexural mobilization is completed using cautery, transitioning from a medial to lateral approach to a lateral to medial approach.

The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt, reflecting the descending colon towards the patient's right lower quadrant. The spleen and omentum are visible as the dissection progresses in a northern direction. The omentocolic attachment is lifted, allowing entry into the lesser sac.

The surgeon performs a lateral-to-medial dissection, dividing the omental colic, splenocolic, lenocolic, phrenocolic, and other attachments to enter the lesser sac. The transverse colon is visible, with the stomach positioned to the left of the screen. Using the third arm of the robotic system, the surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, pushing down the omental colic attachment and other ligaments to fully mobilize the transverse colon.
